subcategory: Pacific Island coastal ornamental poisonous tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun to partial shade in moist to wet, sandy or loamy, slightly acidic to neutral coastal soil in zone 10–12; Fish Poison Tree (Box Fruit, Sea Putat) is a large, ornamental coastal tree native to the beaches and coastal mangroves of the Indo-Pacific region from East Africa through South and Southeast Asia to tropical Australia and the Pacific Islands — bearing spectacular, nocturnal, white to pink-tipped, powder-puff flowers with prominent stamens, followed by distinctive, square-ridged, woody fruits that float in seawater — historically important as a fish stupefier and source of toxic compounds used across the Pacific for traditional fishing.

directions: Plant Barringtonia asiatica in moist, well-drained, sandy coastal soil in full sun; highly tolerant of salt spray and sandy soils; water regularly during establishment; the flowers open at night and fall by morning — best appreciated in the evening. CAUTION: all parts contain toxic saponins and other compounds; seeds and bark were used traditionally across the Pacific to stun fish in tidal pools for harvesting — do not consume. Fish Poison Tree is one of the most architecturally striking coastal trees of the Indo-Pacific — the large (4–6 inch), pompon-like flowers with their dramatic pink-tipped white stamen bundles open at dusk and are gone by morning, attracting nocturnal bats, moths, and hawk moths as pollinators. The fruits are equally distinctive: large (golf ball to baseball sized), square-ridged, four-winged, corky structures that float in seawater and are ocean-dispersed throughout the Indo-Pacific — one of the most remarkable examples of ocean-dispersal in flowering plants. Throughout Oceania, the toxic seed extracts were used for fishing: pounded seeds thrown into tidal pools or reef systems stunned or killed fish within minutes, allowing easy collection. The bark and leaves also have documented traditional medicinal uses across the Pacific Islands for skin conditions and headaches. A magnificent ornamental for sheltered coastal sites in zone 10–12 with evening-opening flowers that create a magical night-garden effect.
sources: Pacific Conservation Biology — Barringtonia coastal ecology

subcategory: Indo-Pacific freshwater mangrove nocturnal flower tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade to full sun in moist to wet, acidic to neutral soil or at the margins of ponds and streams in zone 10–12; Powder Puff Mangrove (Freshwater Mangrove, Wild Guava) is a small to medium tree from the freshwater mangroves, river margins, and coastal wetlands of tropical Africa, Madagascar, South Asia, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific — bearing long, pendant racemes of spectacular, nocturnal, white to pink-tinged, powder-puff flowers with hundreds of long stamens in late night bloom — one of the most beautiful flowering trees of freshwater wetlands.

directions: Plant Barringtonia racemosa in moist to permanently wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun at the margins of ponds, streams, or in any reliably moist lowland position; in dry gardens, regular deep irrigation is needed; highly ornamental when in bloom at night. Powder Puff Mangrove is the freshwater counterpart to Barringtonia asiatica — smaller in overall size, preferring stream banks, pond margins, and freshwater mangrove zones rather than saltwater beaches, but equally spectacular in flower. The long, pendulous flower racemes (each 8–24 inches long) bear dozens of individual flowers that open at night, producing an exquisite display of white, brush-like, many-stamened flowers that fall by morning — the fallen stamens carpeting the ground or water below. The nocturnal blooming strategy reflects pollination by bats and hawk moths; the fragrance is sweet and distinctive. Throughout its vast range across tropical Asia and Africa, the bark, leaves, fruits, and seeds have numerous traditional medicinal applications — used for fever, skin conditions, rheumatism, and as a fish poison (similar to its coastal relative). The fruits are fibrous, oval, and float in fresh water for dispersal along rivers and streams. An outstanding ornamental for tropical water gardens, pond margins, and moist lowland positions in zone 10–12.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, fertile soil in zone 10–12; Baheda (Beleric Myrobalan, Vibhitaki) is a large deciduous tree from the Indian subcontinent, Southeast Asia, and Sri Lanka — one of the three foundational herbs in Triphala (along with Amla and Haritaki), the most important classical formula in Ayurvedic medicine — bearing round to elliptical, grey-green fruits rich in astringent tannins used medicinally for thousands of years in Ayurvedic, Siddha, and traditional Southeast Asian healing systems.

directions: Plant Terminalia bellirica in deep, well-drained, fertile, moderately moist soil in full sun; the tree grows large and needs space to develop its wide-spreading crown; water during establishment and in dry periods, though mature trees are somewhat drought-tolerant. Baheda (vibhitaki in Sanskrit — meaning 'that which removes disease') is one of the three foundational plants in Triphala (Sanskrit: 'three fruits'), the cornerstone polyherbal formula of Ayurvedic medicine used for thousands of years across India, Nepal, and Sri Lanka. Triphala — consisting of equal parts Amla (Phyllanthus emblica), Haritaki (Terminalia chebula), and Baheda (Terminalia bellirica) — is the most widely used compound herb in Ayurveda, prescribed as a rejuvenating tonic, digestive aid, gentle laxative, and general health formula. The dried rind of Baheda fruits is particularly high in tannins (gallic acid, ellagic acid, chebulagic acid) with demonstrated antioxidant, antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and hepatoprotective activity in research studies. Baheda's traditional Ayurvedic indications include respiratory conditions (bronchitis, asthma, cough), hair loss, eye diseases, and digestive disorders. The tree is also valuable for its hard, durable timber and as a shade and avenue tree in India; it produces inconspicuous cream-white flower spikes and later the characteristic ridged, oval grey-green fruits. An important tree for gardens serving South Asian communities in zone 10–12.
sources: Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India — Vibhitaki (Terminalia bellirica)
Journal of Ethnopharmacology — Triphala clinical review

subcategory: Sri Lankan giant monocarpic fan palm

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral deep soil in zone 10–12; Talipot Palm is the largest of all fan palms — native to southern India and Sri Lanka — and produces the largest branched inflorescence of any plant on Earth, bearing 6–8 million individual flowers at once on a terminal spike 20–25 feet tall and 30–40 feet wide, after which the entire palm dies; a remarkable, once-in-a-lifetime natural spectacle requiring 40–80 years to mature.

directions: Plant Talipot Palm in deep, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; it grows relatively quickly once established into an impressive palm that may be the largest fan palm a gardener ever grows; water regularly during establishment and periodically in dry periods; avoid poorly drained sites. Talipot Palm is one of the most extraordinary plants in existence — a palm that spends 40–80 years growing slowly to maturity, accumulating enormous reserves in its massive trunk, then produces the largest inflorescence of any plant on Earth: a single terminal flower spike 20–25 feet tall and 30–40 feet wide, bearing an estimated 6–8 million individual cream-white flowers — then dies. The entire plant's reproductive output is concentrated into this one monumental effort. Native to the moist lowland forests of Sri Lanka and adjacent Kerala, India, the palm was historically of immense cultural importance: the huge leaves (up to 25 feet across) were used for umbrellas (the species name umbraculifera means 'umbrella-bearing'), as writing surfaces (palm leaf manuscripts on which much of South Asian religious literature was recorded), for thatching, and as food service vessels. Growing one in a zone 10–12 garden is an investment in a multi-generational botanical specimen — most planted individuals will never be seen to flower by the person who plants them, but the journey is rewarding in its own right.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Peradeniya, Sri Lanka — Corypha umbraculifera

planting.note: Plant tubers in fall 2–3 inches deep in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, lean, sandy or rocky, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 7–9; provide complete dry summer dormancy; Chilean Nasturtium is a slender, twining, tuberous vine from the mountain scrublands of Chile and Argentina — producing abundant, small (3/4-inch), five-petaled, bright-orange to reddish-orange flowers with a short spur over a long cool growing season — one of the smaller and more cold-hardy of the tuberous Tropaeolum species.

directions: Plant Tropaeolum ciliatum tubers in fall in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic soil; grows through winter and spring in cool, moist conditions; provide a fine trellis, net, or shrub for the slender stems to twine through; withhold all water from June through September (summer drought essential for tuber persistence). Chilean Nasturtium is a delicate, slender, twining vine — more refined in scale than the common garden nasturtium — with small, rounded, peltate leaves and a continuous production of small, five-petaled, bright-orange to brick-red flowers from winter through spring. The vine climbs by twisting petioles (leaf stalks) rather than tendrils, winding gracefully through supporting plants or netting. Native to the scrubby, rocky hillsides of central and southern Chile and adjacent Argentina, it follows the Mediterranean climate pattern: growing in cool, moist winters and dying back completely to its small tuberous root in summer. Like all tuberous Tropaeolum, summer drought is an absolute requirement — even a small amount of summer water can cause the tubers to rot. In zone 7–8, the tubers are adequately cold-hardy in the ground (below frost penetration depth) with good drainage. A charming companion for other winter-growing plants like Oxalis, Ixia, and other Chilean bulbs that share its growing-season preferences.
sources: Universidad de Chile — Chilean flora — Tropaeolum ciliatum

planting.note: Root stem cuttings or plant container stock in spring in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, moist to moderately dry soil with support for the tendrils in zone 8–10; Climbing Gazania is a scandent shrub or climbing perennial from the high-altitude shrub zones and forest edges of the Andes in Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru — bearing large (3–4 inch), brilliant orange-red, daisy-like flowers with gold-yellow centers at the stem tips on plants that climb by modified leaf tendrils through surrounding shrubs — a uniquely beautiful, little-known Andean ornamental.

directions: Plant Mutisia clematis in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; provide shrubs, netting, or a trellis for the modified leaf tendrils to grip; keep evenly moist in the growing season; protect from hard frost below 25°F; propagate from stem cuttings in late spring. Climbing Gazania (Mutisia) is one of the most remarkable genera in the Asteraceae (daisy) family — unlike virtually all other members of the daisy family which are herbaceous or non-climbing, Mutisia species are climbing, tendril-bearing, woody-stemmed scandent shrubs that scramble through Andean cloud forest and shrub margins by means of modified leaf extensions acting as tendrils. The large, solitary daisy-like flowers — brilliant orange-red in M. clematis with a golden-yellow center disc — are produced repeatedly at the stem tips and are genuinely spectacular on close inspection, resembling a large, elegant gerbera daisy held by a climbing vine. The approximately 60 Mutisia species are distributed along the length of the Andes from Venezuela to Patagonia; most are challenging to cultivate but M. clematis is considered one of the more tractable in mild climates. A genuinely unusual and conversation-worthy plant for mild, coastal garden climates in zone 8–10 where cool temperatures, consistent moisture, and good drainage can be provided.
